content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Most people still use AI as if it were Google.
Open a chat.
Ask a question.
Copy the answer.
Paste it somewhere.
Repeat.
That workflow saves minutes.
It doesn’t fundamentally change how you work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Renders BR on UnsplashThe real shift happens when AI stops being something you ask questions to and starts becoming part of your operating model.
That’s the difference between chatting with AI and working alongside it.
For me, that has changed what a one-person company can actually look like.
The real bottleneck isn’t intelligence. It’s execution.
Knowledge workers spend a surprising amount of time producing rather than thinking.
Writing proposals.
Formatting presentations.
Organising documents.
Preparing reports.
Researching competitors.
Summarising meetings.
Cleaning spreadsheets.
Following up emails.
Almost all of these activities are necessary.
Very few require your unique expertise.
Your competitive advantage isn’t formatting slides.
It’s making decisions.
The objective isn’t to complete these tasks faster.
It’s to remove yourself from doing them wherever possible.
⸻
Treat AI like a new employee
Imagine hiring someone on their first day.
You wouldn’t simply say:
“Go write a proposal.”
You would explain:

• how your company works

• * what good work looks like

• * who your customers are

• * what you expect

• * what should never happen
AI is exactly the same.
Most disappointing outputs aren’t caused by weak models.
They’re caused by missing context.
Instead of writing increasingly complicated prompts, invest once in creating permanent context.
Think of it as onboarding your AI teammate.
⸻
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Build a permanent knowledge folder
Create one folder that becomes the operating manual for your AI.
Mine contains three simple categories.
1. Personal Operating Guide
Explain how you think.
Describe your role.
Define quality.
List your non-negotiables.
Explain how you make decisions.
The goal isn’t autobiography.
It’s helping AI understand how work gets approved.
⸻
2. Company Strategy
Document:

• annual objectives

• * target customers

• * current priorities

• * projects in progress

• * things you’re deliberately not pursuing
When AI understands your direction, its recommendations become dramatically more relevant.
⸻
3. Writing Standards
Most AI writing sounds recognisable because the same patterns appear repeatedly.
Build a document listing:

• phrases you dislike

• * words you never use

• * preferred formatting

• * paragraph length

• * tone of voice
Update it every time you notice something that feels artificial.
Over time the model starts sounding much more like you.
⸻
Let AI ask questions
One habit transformed my workflow more than any prompt.
Instead of immediately producing an answer, ask AI to interview you first.
Good employees clarify before they execute.
AI should too.
Rather than guessing your intent, it should gather enough information to produce the right result the first time.
That usually saves far more time than writing increasingly detailed prompts.
⸻
Build reusable systems, not reusable prompts
People obsess over prompt libraries.
They’re solving the wrong problem.
What scales isn’t prompts.
It’s process.
Whenever AI produces something excellent, don’t just save the document.
Save the structure.
Proposal templates.
Research frameworks.
Meeting summaries.
Workshop agendas.
Campaign briefs.
Those become reusable operating systems rather than one-off outputs.
Over time you stop starting from blank pages.
⸻
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Vadim Artyukhin on UnsplashUse your voice, not your keyboard
Typing often compresses your thinking.
Speaking expands it.
When I dictate ideas instead of typing them, I naturally include:

• context

• nuance

• examples

• priorities

• uncertainty
All of that helps AI produce dramatically better work.
Whether you use built-in dictation or dedicated speech-to-text software doesn’t matter.
The richer the input, the stronger the output.
⸻
Keep context lean
One mistake many people make is continuously adding more information.
More context doesn’t always produce better results.
Large context files slow everything down and increase costs.
Instead:

• keep core documents concise

• * remove outdated information

• * update strategy quarterly

• * archive completed projects
Treat your knowledge base like a living operating manual.
⸻
Review like a CEO, not an assistant
The biggest mindset change is this:
Stop trying to make AI perfect.
Start managing it.
Great leaders don’t personally write every document.
They review.
Challenge assumptions.
Improve decisions.
Approve outcomes.
That’s exactly how AI should fit into your workflow.
Execution becomes delegated.
Judgement stays with you.
⸻
Build specialists
General-purpose AI is useful.
Specialised AI is transformative.
Instead of one assistant that does everything reasonably well, create dedicated workflows for recurring responsibilities.
One for research.
One for marketing.
One for proposal writing.
One for reporting.
Each follows the same process every time, producing consistent outputs with far less supervision.
Eventually, you’re no longer prompting.
You’re managing a team of digital specialists.
⸻
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by BoliviaInteligente on UnsplashAutomate the predictable
Once a workflow becomes repetitive, remove yourself from it.
Morning briefings.
Weekly reports.
Meeting preparation.
Content research.
Competitive monitoring.
Status updates.
If it follows the same pattern every week, it probably shouldn’t require your attention every week.
Automation isn’t about replacing work.
It’s about eliminating repetitive work.
⸻
Where human value remains
AI changes execution.
It doesn’t replace judgement.
Customers still buy trust.
Teams still need leadership.
Partnerships still depend on relationships.
Strategy still requires experience.
The more production work AI absorbs, the more valuable distinctly human capabilities become.
That’s where founders, consultants and executives create leverage.
⸻
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Olegs Jonins on UnsplashThe One-Person Company Isn’t Really One Person
People talk about “solopreneurs” as if they’re doing everything alone.
The reality is different.
Today’s one-person company can have:

• an AI researcher

• an AI writer

• an AI analyst

• an AI project coordinator

• an AI assistant

• an AI reporting specialist
None of them replace the founder.
They multiply the founder.
The result isn’t working harder.
It’s spending more time on the work that only you can do.
That’s the real promise of AI – not faster typing, but better leverage.
